title: Lamb tagine with apricots
subtitle: Slow-braised lamb with dried apricots, almond, and ras el hanout.
excerpt: Lamb shoulder slow-braised with onion, ras el hanout, and a generous handful of dried apricots and toasted almond. The classic Moroccan sweet-savoury tagine, eaten with couscous and a fresh herb salad.
sourceNotes: The Moroccan sweet-tagine tradition documented in regional cookery from Fez, Marrakech, and the Sous Valley. The dried-fruit-with-meat technique descends from the Andalusian and Berber kitchens.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to 3 months. Defrost overnight, reheat gently and add a splash of water to loosen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Improves with a day in the fridge. Make ahead and reheat to serve.

glossaryTerms.definition: A Moroccan spice blend whose name means "top of the shop" — the best of what the seller has. The exact mix varies by family and souk, but expects cinnamon, ginger, allspice, coriander, cumin, cardamom, clove, nutmeg, and rose. A good one is fragrant and warm without being hot.

content.text: A tagine is both a vessel and a dish. The conical lid traps the steam and condenses it back over the meat, producing a tender braise from very little added liquid. A heavy lidded casserole works as well. The dish is the slow conversation between lamb shoulder, ras el hanout, sweet dried apricot, and a final scattering of toasted almond.

The apricots cook to a glossy, soft texture that almost melts into the gravy. Use unsulphured dried apricots if you can find them; the supermarket bright-orange ones are over-treated and turn the dish a similar shade.
A heavy lidded Dutch oven or a proper Moroccan tagine, both around 4 to 5 litres. The flat hob underneath the tagine needs a diffuser plate to spread the heat.

Pat {{lamb-shoulder}} of lamb dry. Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in the Dutch oven over medium-high. Brown the lamb in 3 batches, 3 minutes a side, lifting each batch onto a plate. Don't crowd the pan.

Drop the heat to medium. Add {{onion}} of chopped onion. Cook 10 minutes until soft and golden. Stir in {{garlic}} of garlic, {{ginger-root}} of ginger, and {{ras-el-hanout}} of ras el hanout for 1 minute. Drop in the {{cinnamon-stick}} of cinnamon stick. Pour in {{tinned-tomatoes}} of chopped tomatoes and cook 3 minutes, mashing with a wooden spoon.

Return the lamb to the pot with any juices. Pour in {{stock-chicken}} of stock and the saffron water. Stir in {{honey}} of honey, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{black-pepper}} of pepper. Bring to a simmer. Cover, drop the heat to its lowest, and cook for 90 minutes.

Lift the lid. Add {{dried-apricots}} of dried apricots. Cook uncovered for another 30 minutes until the lamb is fully tender and the sauce has reduced to a thick gravy that coats the meat. Stir occasionally to keep the bottom from catching.

While the tagine finishes, set a small dry frying pan over medium heat. Tip in {{almonds-flaked}} of flaked almonds and toast for 3 minutes, shaking the pan, until pale gold. Tip onto a plate at once or they will continue cooking on the hot metal.

Rest the tagine 15 minutes off the heat. Lift out the cinnamon stick. Taste; the sauce should sit between savoury, warm, and softly sweet. Scatter with the toasted almonds, {{coriander}} of chopped coriander, and {{parsley-flat}} of chopped parsley. Serve with couscous, flatbread, or a herb-and-onion salad.

items.symptom: Sauce too thin at the end
items.cause: Lid kept on for the apricot stage.
items.fix: Uncover for the last 30 minutes so the sauce reduces. The dish should sit thick on the spoon.

items.cause: Heat too high during the braise.
items.fix: Use the lowest possible simmer for the 90 minutes covered. The bubble should be barely perceptible. A heat-diffuser helps.

items.cause: Too much honey, or apricots too dominant.
items.fix: Use unsulphured apricots; supermarket bright-orange ones are over-treated and oversweet. Adjust honey to taste; a squeeze of lemon at the end balances.

items.cause: Old spice blend, or not bloomed in fat.
items.fix: Use a fresh blend from a deli or Middle Eastern grocer. Stir into the hot onion-ginger mix for a full minute before liquid goes in.

content.text: Lamb tagine with apricots is a celebration dish from Fez and Marrakech, served at Eid and at weddings, the kind of tagine that takes the proud cook a full afternoon. The sweet-savoury combination comes from the Andalusian-Berber kitchen brought across the strait centuries ago. Serve with mint tea after, never wine.
